BISHOP MURPHY—THE MAN AND HIS BOOKS.

In the thirties and forties of the last century ‘‘ Tours in Ireland,”’
by various writers, made their appearance almost annually. In one of
these tours, which I think must be extremely scarce, for I know of no
copy cxoept that in my own possession, entitled *“ Journal of a Tour in
Ircland,”” during themonths of October and November, 1835, by
G.E.G.M., and printed *for p1'-imate circulation only,”” London, 1836,
there are a few short and interesting references to the library of this
book-loving Bishop which are worth quoting. The tourist made it a
practice to visit the churches and schools of all denominations -and,
Americanlike, to introduce himself unceremoniously to the clergy and
others. The day following his arrival in the City of Cork (7th Oct.) he
notes :

*“ My next endeavour was to find out where Dr. Murphy, the Roman
Bishop of Cork, lived ; and after wading ankle-deep in mud and slime

aud filth, of all sorts, in the midst of rain, I reached his house. It
adjoing a chapel, the largest in the town, and by some, 1 believe,
yeleped a cathedral; but as little like one in reality as his abode is
like a palace. Wretched hovels composed: the rest of the street, but
. the house itself is convenient enough in point of size. A dirty maid-
8ervant opened the door, and informed me that the bishop was staymg
at Black Rock, at his sister’s, and not expected home all that week.”’
Nﬁbhlng daunted, he pursued the bishop, and eventually tracked him

down, and again wrote, ‘‘ He spoke of books with great enthusiasm,

bemg himself a sort of bibliomaniac, and said he had a great desire to
bequeath his I brary to the City of Cork, provided he could only pro-

Cure g guitable building.” How one regrets that there was no Car- .

tion, and so have saved the city the irreparable loss of such a library|
The tourist afterwards spent an hour in the bishop’s library, ‘‘ which
fills up every room in his house. It appeared to be a very miscellaneous,
rather than a choica or valuable one; bought cheap at auctions prin-
cipally, and by no m:ans confined to divinmty.”

Here is a pleasant pen plcturen of the Bishop taken from a recent
issue of the ‘ Dublin Evening Telegraph ™ :—

Great excitement and much pleasure did every visit of the good
Bishop cause among Dublin old-book sellers, whether they rented shops,
or enjoyed free standings along the quays. There was arranging and
dusting of volumes, and goings backwards and forwards, and frequent
questionings as to the hour of his arrival. At last, the anxious guardian
of past literature is gladdened by the apparition of the gold-headed
care, the silk stockings fitting in the buckl.d shoes, the waistcoat mot
innocent of snuff, and loose coat, the broad-brimmed hat, and the kind
good-natured face under it. If the bookseller had wife and children—
and what secondhand dealer in books is unprovided with these allies—
they presented themselves to receive his blessing, and cordially did he
give 1t. Then went on for an hour or two, according to circumstanoces,
a succession of enhancing, and cheapening, and joking, for the good
Bishop could afford to joke. If a price was asked which he affected 0
think too high, he would stop short, gaze ludicro-sternly over his

pectac]es at the culprit, and cry out, ‘“ Ah, you think to impose on
the poor-Connaughtman.’”’” He made up his bill as he went along ; and
when ‘he left the shop, he left behind him cheerful hearts and some-
thing to meet the ment or the auctioneer’s bill. The words of the old
song might be appropriately applied to the kind-hearted Doctor, H'B

brought the summer along with him.”

But this note would be incomplﬂsl:e if it did not include the last
words penned by the tourist before issuing his book. They are to
bo found in his introduction, which embraces a general summing Up of
the politics and polemics of the day. *‘ The first Roman Bishop whol
I met with,” he writes, ** was all life, activity and vigilance: rich, bub
spending little on himself; much on books, schools and other public
objects. They complained, and justly, of hard work. They had nov
¢ime for study or for improvement.”’

Rev. Thomas Roche, of St. Patrick’s Collz ge, Maynooth, has been
good enough to give us the following account of the MSS. preserved
there. ‘‘There are about 120 volumes of MSS. bequeathed by Bishop
Murphy ; most were written by scribes, the majority of whom belonged
to the O’Longain family, of whom a worthy descendant Father Mac-
Sweeney is 1n our College. The others are in the autograph of various
scribes. There is ore by the poet Piaras MacGearailt (vide, his
‘“Poems ”’ edited by R. O’Foghludha). These 120 volumes contain all
surts of matter, prose and poetry, sacred and profane, some purely
literary, others historical, and the book-plate of the Bishop is on the
covers of them all. But I might write for a day and not describe them.
Iiven after four years I have not seen them all. A catalogue i1s in
preparation, and we are well on with it; but I cannot say when it
will be ready. We <hall not dclay, and perhaps the end of the year will
gee 1t off our hands.”

JUSTIN McCARTHY.
This distinguished Irish novelist, journalist, historian and states-

man died at Folkestone on 24th April, in his 82nd year. Born

in Cork, 22nd November, 1830, and educated locally, he commenced
his long literary care:r of over sixty years on the ‘“ Cork Examiner.”
In 1853 he went to Liverpool, on the staff of the ‘‘ Northern Times,”
and in 1855 married Miss Charlotte Allman, a member of a well-known
Cork family. He removed to London in 1860 to represent ‘‘ The Morn-

ing Star ”’ in the Reporters’ Gallery, and was shortly afterwards

appointed foreign editor, as he could translate from many languages,
an accomplishment mainly self acquired, and in 1864 was appointed
chief editor. When the ‘“ Star’’ disappeared in 1868, McCarthy went
to America, visiting many of the States and studying the political and
social systems, besides writing and lecturing. He returned to England
in 1870, and joined the staff of the Daily News’’ as a leader writer,
and whilst so engeged was elected M.P. for Longford in 1879. When,
In 1890, Mr. Parnell was deposed from the leadership of the Irish

P&I*ty, Mbp. McCarthy was chosen as his successor, and filled the post |

until 1896. As he never allowed his political position to interfere with
his literary pursuits, the double drain proved too much for a constitu-
bion never over stromg, and a serious illness followed and his life
Was despaired of. Retiring to Westgate, he recuperated wonder-

continued up to Christmas last. Much of his work must inevitably
perish—his very facility militated against the quality of it. But his
average was unquestionably high, and most of his historical studies
will live. Although nearly the whole of his working life was spent in
England, he never lost touch with his own country or people, and all
who knew him loved him, and recognised in him a sincere patriot, a
steady friend and a sterling Irish gentleman. His only son i1s Justin
Huutly McCarthy, the well-known novelist and playwright.  Our ;
veteran correspondent, Mr. James Hayes, of IEnnis, writes to us to say
that he and Mr. McCarthy were both born in Dunmanway, and were
life-long friemds.

- On March 23rd, Sir James D. LaTouche being in the chair, Miss
Eleanor Hull delivered a lecture with the title *“ An Irish Paradise
Lost.”” The poem, which was the subject of her notice, was the Saltair

na Rann, or ‘¢ Psalter of the Virses,” so named because it was divided:

into 150 parts, like the Psalms of David. Its composition was at
tributed to Oengus the Culdee, who lived about the beginmng of the
ninth century, but it was undoubtcdly later, and previous events
were mentioned in the poem itself which would bring it as late as
1050-1100. The amcient poetry, Miss Hull said, was characterised by

ruggid and brilliant thought and expression and by great complexity of

metre ;- both the writers and the illuminators of Early Ireland showed
gifts of patience and arduous labour which the Gael of the present day

was suppesed to be dcficient in. There never was a time when Ireland

did not produce beautiful verse, and it was specially true that there
never was a time when poetry failcd among the anonymous peasant
singers. Miss Hull said the «Saltair na Rann ”’ was undoubtedly the
greatest religious poem of Ancient Ireland, and had only becen over-
Jooked because it has never been translated. It desoribed the Creation
of the Universe, heaven and hell, the fall of Lucifer, the E'a.rﬂ?*l!
Paradise, the fall and penance of Adam and Eve, and then gave a briéf
Bkerbbh of biblical history to the coming of Christ, ending with the
Resurrection. The account of the Creation, she said, showed many

curious and oriental features. The earth was referred to as round, com~

Miss Hull quoted largely from the portions of the poem describing
Paradise and the Penance of Adam and Eve. She said there was a
freshness and simplicity about these Irish poems not usually to be
found in medizeval religious poetry, and which even where the Irish poet
was writing over a Latin original, as in parts of this poem, imparted
to it quite a new and Gaelic flavour. As an example of this she read
the account of the devil’s interview with the sempent outside Paradise,
suggesting that it was a great indignity that a creature of so much in-
telligence as he should be kept outside in the fields whem Adam and
Eve, who were created later than the animals and were therefore
younger, should be in possession of the garden. They make an alliance
to break down the privileges accorded to Xve and proceed ‘‘in their
twoness,”’ i.e., the devil in the serpent’s body, to the gate of Paradise,
where they call upon Eve to come and converse with them. She replies
that she has no time to converse with anyone, as Adam is gone up to
Heaven to make adoration to the Almighty, and she is left alone to
feed the animals. She complains very much of being left to do this
menial task, and by a skilful playirg upon har feelings of discontent
and jealousy, the serpent at length persuades hér to open the gate and

IRISH GRAMMAR AND DicrioNarRy. What Grammar and what Dic.
tionary would you recommend to one who wished to gain a reading
knowledge of the Irish language for historical purposes? I notice that
Gill & Son, of Dublin, publish many text books on Irish, but I am at
a loss to know which to order? With congratulations on your admir-
able little review, I am, sincerely yours,

| FREDERICK ALDRICH CLEVELAND.
Bryn Mawr, Pennsylvania, U.S.A.

* % O’Reilly’s Dictionary is the best general one for Early Modern
Irish, and helps also with Middle Irish. Dineen’s larger Irish Diction-
ary 1> best for Modern Irish. But it depends on the age of the pieces
and the kind of work Mr. Cleveland wants to do, what texts and books
are best to recommend. As for grammars, I have myself found the little
grammar of the Christian Brothers (to be got at Gill’s) useful for
Modern Irish, and Thurveysen’s Grammar is the standard one for Old
and Middle Irish. For Middle Irish the best Glossary is that attached
to the texts published in ‘‘ Irische Texte,”” Vol. I., and for Harly-
Modern Irish the Glossary attached to Atkinson’s edition of Keating’s
‘“ Three Shafts of Death’’ ig excellent. If I can help your corres-
pondent further, privately, I will gladly do so. ELEANOR HULL.
14, Stanley Gardens, Notting Hill, W. | | |

FORTHCOMING WORKS.

In a few days will be published by Hodder and Stoughton an import-
ant work entitled ‘‘ The New Irish Constitution.”’” Several distinguished
men have collaborated in this book, which is under the general editor-
ship of Professor J. H. Morgan. Mrs. J. R. Grcen writes on ‘‘Irish
Nationality,”” Professor Pollard on ‘‘ Ireland as a De-pendency,” Mr.
Jonathan Pim, K.C., on ‘' The Irish Land Question.” Contemporary
Ireland from the religious point of view is treated by Monsignor
O’Riordan, Rev. Canon Courtenay Moore (Church of Ireland), Rev.
J. B. Armour (Presbyterian), and Rev. W. Crawford, M.A. (formerly
principal of Wesley College, Dublin)

Miss Eleanor Hull will publish with Chatto and Wmdus 1n the early
autumn a new volume entitled ‘‘ The Poem Book of the Gael.” It con-
tains translations of Irish Gaelic poetry of all periods, and will be a
representative collection of Irish verse, religious, patriotic and elegaic.
It will be adorned with initial letters and designs from Irish MSS., and
most of the translations will be in English verse.

Mr. M. McDonnell Bodkin, K.C., has wrnitten a popular history of
“ Grattan’s Parliament: Before and After,”’ which is to come from Mr.
Fisher Unwin. The history is mainly concerned with the rise and
dramatic fiall of the old Irish Parliament, but 1ts opening chapters con-
tain a brief summary of previous Parliaments, and it conclud:s with an
account of the long struggle which succeeded the Union and finds its

- climax to-day.

- Messrs. Jack announce that a volume on Home Rule by Mr. L. G.
Redmond Howard will be included in the second dozen of ‘‘ The People’s
Books.”” There will be a preface by Mr. Robert Harcourt.

Messrs. M. H. Gill & Son will shortly publish ‘‘ The Neighbourhood
of Dublin : Its Topography Antiquities and Historical Associations,’’ by
Mr. Weston St. John Joyce. It will be profusely illustrated and con-
tain an introduction by the veteran, Dr. P. W. Joyce.
